'''Scott Hill''' (born 30 May 1977) is an Australian former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played in the 1990s and 2000s. He was previously with the [[London Broncos#2006–2011: Harlequins Rugby League|Harlequins RL]] club in the [[Super League]]. His usual position is at {{rlp|fe}}. Hill previously played in Australia for the [[Melbourne Storm]], [[Hunter Mariners]] (one of only two players to play in all the club's games in their lone season) and the [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]].  Hill is a former [[New South Wales Rugby League team|New South Wales]] and [[Australia national rugby league team|Australian]] representative player.

==Playing career==

===Early career===
Originally from [[Dubbo]],<ref>{{cite news |last1=Griffiths |first1=Bethany |title=The country towns that produced Australian sporting stars |work=The Weekly Times |publisher=Nationwide News Pty Ltd |date=8 September 2021 |location=Melbourne, Victoria}}</ref> Hill was playing with the Forster Tuncurry Hawks as a 15-year-old when he was spotted by [[Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs]] talent scouts. At the time he was undertaking a carpentry apprenticeship and opted to remain with his junior club, where he would play under the coaching of [[Dennis Tutty]] in the period when the Forster Tuncurry Hawks would win back-to-back [[Clayton Cup]] titles.<ref name="ARPodcast"/>  
In 1995, he would sign a [[Super League (Australia)|Super League]] contract and was allocated to the new [[Hunter Mariners]] franchise ahead of the 1996 season. However, when Super League was banned from starting in 1997, he would join the Bulldogs, where he was set to replace the retiring [[Terry Lamb]].<ref name="DT2021">{{cite news |title=Blast from the Past |work=The Daily Telegraph|date=7 May 2021 |location=Sydney, New South Wales |page=101}}</ref>

===Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs===
During the 1996 season, Hill was playing with the Bulldogs' junior squad where he was described as a "two iron with ears".<ref name="ARPodcast"/> He was elevated by head coach [[Chris Anderson (rugby)|Chris Anderson]], to make his first grade debut for Canterbury-Bankstown against [[South Sydney Rabbitohs|South Sydney]] in round 18 of the [[1996 ARL season]] scoring two tries in a 28–4 victory at the [[Sydney Football Stadium (1988)|Sydney Football Stadium]].<ref name="ARPodcast"/> He would play in five first grade matches for the Bulldogs, featuring on the winning side in all five occasions.<ref name="DT2021"/>

===Hunter Mariners===
With Super League commencing in 1997, Hill moved to the [[Hunter Mariners]] for the [[1997 Super League (Australia) season|1997 Super League season]], completing his carpentry apprenticeship with teammate [[Robbie McCormack]] during the preseason.<ref name="ARPodcast">{{cite podcast |url=https://open.spotify.com/episode/4kapv7HgASi79xFjcL0f7G?si=3fd27d8f9a444a6e |title=Ep 436. The Legends Series – Scott Hill – Footy Hurts & Helps! (Pt 1) |website=andyraymondunfiltered.com.au |host=Andy Raymond |date=9 April 2023 |time= |access-date=12 April 2023}}</ref> He would score a try in the Hunter Mariners final ever Super League match in round 18 against [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks|Cronulla-Sutherland]] at the [[Topper Stadium]].<ref>{{cite book |last1=Middleton |first1=David |title=Rugby League 1998 |date=January 1998 |publisher=Harper Sports |location=Sydney|isbn=0732264243}}</ref> Hill is one of just two players to have played in every game for the Mariners,<ref name="BowenProfile">{{cite news |title=Legends of League |work=Bowen Independent |date=19 May 2017 |location=Bowen, Queensland |page=14}}</ref> including the [[1997 World Club Championship]] final, which would prove to be the final Mariners game.<ref name="DT2021"/>

===Melbourne Storm===
Recruited by Melbourne, Hill was among several players from the club's inaugural season in [[1998 Melbourne Storm season|1998]]. He would miss Melbourne's [[1999 NRL grand final]] win over the [[St. George Illawarra Dragons]] due to a [[knee reconstruction]].<ref>{{cite news | last = Fuller| first = Mark| title = Grand final a Hill too far| work = The Age| date = 2006-10-02| url = http://www.theage.com.au/news/league/grand-final-a-hill-too-far/2006/10/01/1159641213500.html| accessdate = 2009-12-16}}</ref> Having won the 1999 Premiership, the [[Melbourne Storm]] travelled to England to contest the [[2000 World Club Challenge]] against [[Super League]] Champions [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]], with Hill playing at {{rlp|fe}} and scoring two tries in the victory.

Hill would play more than 170 matches for Melbourne, with his last match coming when he started at {{rlp|fe}} in Melbourne's [[2006 NRL Grand Final]] loss to the [[Brisbane Broncos]].<ref name="BowenProfile"/><ref name="ARPodcast2">{{cite podcast |url=https://open.spotify.com/episode/51PAc8PSFz7I6q8Hhf0EA7?si=y0V7RqXYRD2CLYnO5AxV6Q |title=Ep 437. The Legends Series – Scott Hill – Footy Hurts & Helps! (Pt 2) |website=andyraymondunfiltered.com.au |host=Andy Raymond |date=11 April 2023 |time= |access-date=12 April 2023}}</ref>

===Harlequins===
Leaving at the end of that season, Hill moved to London to play for the [[London Broncos#2006–2011: Harlequins Rugby League|Harlequins]] club of [[Super League]],<ref name="RLPHill">{{cite web |title=Rugby League Project – Scott Hill |url=https://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/players/scott-hill/summary.html |publisher=Rugby League Project |access-date=25 October 2022}}</ref> where he would play two seasons before retiring at the end of the 2008 season.<ref name="RLPHill"/>

==Representative career==
Hill was selected to represent [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]] in the [[2000 Anzac Test]], coming off the bench in Australia's 52–0 whitewash of New Zealand. Following this selection, Hill would play for [[New South Wales rugby league team|New South Wales]] in the [[2000 State of Origin series]]. He would play all three games in NSW's 3–0 series victory.<ref name="DT2021"/>

At the end of the [[2000 NRL season]], Hill would be selected for the Australian squad for the [[2000 Rugby League World Cup]],<ref name="DT2021"/> scoring two tries in Australia's 66–10 win over Samoa in the quarter-finals. Hill was the starting {{rlp|lk}} in Australia's 40–20 win over New Zealand in the [[2000 Rugby League World Cup final|final]]. He would make three further appearances for Australia, with his final international match in 2004.

During his career, Hill also played four representative matches for [[Country New South Wales rugby league team|NSW Country]] in the then annual [[City vs Country Origin]] match.<ref name="RLPHill"/>

==Personal life==
After retirement from professional rugby league, Hill would later be involved in a mentoring business for young rugby league athletes.<ref>{{cite news |last1=Bruce |first1=Mike |title=Team spirits shine |work=The Sunday Mail |publisher=Nationwide News Pty Ltd |date=3 June 2012 |location=Brisbane, Queensland |page=6 (UonSunday)}}</ref>
